title: Semi-Supervised Gaze Estimation via Disentangled Subspace Contrastive Learning
abstract: Appearance-based gaze estimation always suffers from poor generalization due to limited annotated samples and insufficient dataset diversity. Leading approaches adopt weakly supervised learning to generate large-scale pseudo-labeled data from unconstrained real-world scenarios, aiming to mitigate the domain shifts. In this work, we devise a simple yet effective semi-supervised learning architecture that leverages unlabeled data to enhance domain generalization, thereby reducing reliance on labor-intensive manual annotations. Our key insight is to impose Jacobian regularization to disentangle feature representations into discriminative subspaces dedicated to specific gaze components, such as pitch and yaw angles. We further exploit the intrinsic ordinal ranking within each subspace for contrastive learning, enabling the model to learn robust gaze representations from a small set of labeled samples and an abundance of unlabeled ones. This ultimately yields our Disentangled Subspace Contrastive Learning (DSCL) framework. Extensive experiments on multiple benchmarks verify that the proposed DSCL is plug-and-play, achieving competitive performance using only 20\%, 10\%, and even 5\% of the annotated data under both in-domain and cross-domain evaluation settings. The public code is available at \href{https://github.com/da60266/DSCL}{https://github.com/da60266/DSCL}.
decision_comment:
This paper proposes a novel method for disentangled self-supervised learning for gaze estimation, which leads to performance gains for in and cross domain evaluation settings under low-label conditions (20%, 10% and 5%). It received scores of weak accept, weak accept, weak accept and weak reject.

The AC carefully read the paper, and all discussion between the reviewers and authors. Additionally, the AC disregarded the review by Reviewer TKKj (weak reject) in rendering their final decision. Overall, there was consensus among the reviewers that the proposed method is mathematically sound, novel, and well-grounded in theoretical insights that are useful to the machine learning community, beyond this current work. However the one major weakness of the paper is that it is not as well positioned to advance the literature on gaze estimation in the absolute sense of accuracy. The AC agrees with the reviewers that the paper would have been a stronger contribution to gaze estimation research if the authors had conducted more thorough experiments to evaluate the performance of their technique with 100% labels of existing gaze datasets together with large unlabeled dataset, for example, identically to OmniGaze along with showing comparisons to OmniGaze as well. The lack of this result weakens the contribution of this work with respect to research on gaze estimation. Hence a better positioning of this work would then have been to frame its major contribution as a general machine learning methodology for SSL for regression tasks, with multiple regression outputs which render the ordering of the latent space ambiguous, with gaze estimation being, one among many other, one downstream application of the general methodology.

Notwithstanding its limitations, all in all, the AC feels that the paper is slightly above the acceptance threshold for ICML in terms of its methodological contributions and for that, the AC recommends Weak accept (low priority: accept if there is room in the program).

reviews.summary: This work tackles the problem of semi-supervised learning for 3D gaze estimation. The key idea is to take advantage of large amounts of unlabeled data, which is achieved by an orthogonal Jacobian regularization, which forces the learned feature vectors to represent yaw and pitch in a disentangled manner. Supervised regression, unsupervised/supervised contrastive losses and a ranking loss are trained jointly. The paper does evaluation in a within-dataset and across dataset scenario, showing competitive results when
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
**Strengths**
- Excellent presentation -- the paper is well written and reasonably easy to follow with clear diagrams and visualizations
- Originality - applying contrastive techniques, which have proven effective in the recognition domain for learning representations with limited labels, makes sense for this problem domain as well. The formulation to disentangle features for yaw and pitch makes sense.
- In the evaluation setting of taking an existing dataset and "pretending" that a portion of it does not have labels, the proposed approach has good performance.
- The ablation studies are thorough and demonstrate the effectiveness of the proposed regularization technique

**Weaknesses**
- The main weakness of this approach is that while it is semi-supervised, it only evaluates by assuming fully labeled datasets have less labels. A much more appropriate demonstration would be to use a large corpus of unlabeled data, and then show that the proposed idea allows for better performance than any supervised method on all the labeled data that is available. This would demonstrate that at a large scale the proposed idea really makes a big difference.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
(1) Why is the evaluation done by taking labeled datasets, and pretending that their labels don't exist? Because of this, the method is shown as more of a proof-of-concept, rather than an approach that's proven to work at a large scale. A better evaluation setup would take existing labeled datasets and train on all of them as a baseline, and then add a very large amount of unlabeled data for semi supervised training. If in this case the performance is stronger, then this is a clear win and a very strong contribution.

(2) Could the authors please articulate what is the significance of the approach, given that it can't outperform fully supervised methods on the same datasets? Similar to the previous point, why would one want to train on less labels to obtain competitive or slightly worse performance, when they could train on all the labels from the dataset?

reviews.summary: The paper proposes DSCL, a semi-supervised gaze estimation framework that combines Jacobian-based feature disentanglement with subspace-wise contrastive learning. The main idea is to separate latent features into gaze-component-specific subspaces (e.g., pitch and yaw), and then exploit both labeled and unlabeled data via supervised/unsupervised contrastive objectives and pseudo-ranking. The motivation is reasonable, especially the claim that scalar ranking is ambiguous for multi-target regression, and the method is clearly described.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths

1.The paper addresses a relevant problem: reducing reliance on labeled gaze data by using semi-supervised learning.

2.The disentanglement motivation is reasonable. The argument that pitch/yaw regression may suffer from ambiguity under a single scalar ranking is interesting, and the proposed subspace-wise treatment is conceptually clean. 

3.The method is relatively lightweight and plug-and-play in the sense that it can be added to multiple backbones and consistently improves them under reduced-label settings. Table 1 and Table 2 do show improvements over corresponding baselines in many few-label settings.

Weaknesses
1.The experimental benchmark coverage is insufficient.
The paper evaluates on only three datasets: Gaze360, MPIIGaze, and EyeDiap. For a paper making claims about generalized gaze estimation and practical low-label learning, the absence of ETH-XGaze is a notable omission. ETH-XGaze is one of the most standard and challenging large-scale benchmarks in this area, and excluding it weakens the empirical credibility of the paper’s generalization claims. 

2.The overall performance is not particularly strong relative to stronger existing methods.
While DSCL improves the authors’ baseline and some prior models in low-label settings, the absolute performance is still often behind stronger domain generalization/adaptation baselines. For example, in the cross-domain results, methods such as PnP-GA, UnReGA, and CRGA achieve clearly better full-label numbers than the simple baseline family used here. Even after adding DSCL, the resulting numbers do not clearly establish a new state of the art; rather, they show that DSCL is a useful add-on for weaker baselines. 

3.The main empirical story is more “improves a baseline under low-label settings” than “competitive with the best methods.”
The paper repeatedly claims competitive performance with 20%, 10%, and 5% labels, but in several tables this competitiveness is mostly with respect to the authors’ own baseline or selected few-label variants, rather than the strongest available methods under broader evaluation protocols. 

4.Moreover, the paper does not evaluate a stronger semi-supervised setting with full labeled data plus additional external unlabeled data. Such a setting would be important to verify whether DSCL is genuinely useful beyond the limited-label regime, rather than mainly serving as a low-label regularization technique
reviews.final_justification: Thanks to the authors' response and I raise my score for their efforts to reslove my concern.

reviews.summary: This paper proposes Disentangled Subspace Contrastive Learning (DSCL), a semi-supervised framework for appearance-based gaze estimation designed to reduce reliance on large annotated datasets. The method first applies Jacobian regularization to a small set of labeled data, which forces the extracted features to disentangle into orthogonal subspaces dedicated to specific gaze components, such as pitch and yaw. Following this disentanglement, the framework leverages abundant unlabeled data by calculating feature similarity matrices within these isolated subspaces and applying spectral seriation to generate pseudo-ranking orders. These ranking orders serve as implicit supervisory signals to guide unsupervised contrastive learning. Experimental evaluations across the Gaze360, MPIIGaze, and EyeDiap datasets demonstrate that DSCL functions as a plug-and-play module, achieving predictive performance comparable to fully supervised methods while utilizing only 5% to 20% of the available ground-truth labels.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
Strengths:  
1. The paper is technically sound and backs its empirical design with formal mathematical proofs. In Appendix A, the authors formally prove why traditional scalar ranking fails for multi-target regression (rank ambiguity) and demonstrate via Theorem 3.2 how Jacobian regularization induces column-sparsity to create orthogonal feature subspaces.  
2. The framework utilizes unlabeled data highly efficiently, achieving competitive results with only 5% to 20% of annotated data.
3. The framework adds no extra parameters during inference and integrates easily into existing state-of-the-art models (e.g., PureGaze, PnP-GA, UnReGA), consistently improving their performance.  
4. The empirical validation is extensive and well-designed. The authors evaluate the model across in-domain, cross-domain (including Domain Generalization, Unsupervised Domain Adaptation, and Supervised Domain Adaptation), and low-diversity scenarios.  

Weaknesses:
1. The method relies on an argmax operation to generate the binary mask matrix $\mathcal{M}$. As the authors honestly note, this blocks gradient propagation through the mask generation path, making it a "constant filter" during backpropagation rather than a dynamically learnable routing mechanism. This severely couples the model's success to the supervised pre-training phase; if the pre-training fails to find a good initial mask, the subsequent semi-supervised learning cannot easily recover.  
2. While the combination is novel, the underlying building blocks are not entirely new. For instance, the core semi-supervised contrastive learning mechanism utilizing spectral seriation is heavily derived from Dai et al. (2023a) . The originality lies primarily in the application and the "disentanglement" precursor, rather than proposing a fundamentally new unsupervised learning paradigm.  
3. In the experimental section (specifically Section 4.3.1 and Table 2), the authors compare DSCL against various state-of-the-art methods such as PureGaze, PnP-GA, and UnReGA. The paper lacks a dedicated "Baselines" subsection to introduce these compared methods. While the text briefly mentions categories like Domain Generalization (DG), Unsupervised Domain Adaptation (UDA), and Supervised Domain Adaptation (SDA) accompanied by bulk citations, it would be better to map the specific method names used in the tables to these categories.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors: In the experimental section, some methods (such as CA-Net in table 1) only report their performance when trained on the full labeled dataset ("Full" setting). Why are the results for different labeled data ratios (e.g., 20%, 10%, 5%) omitted for these specific methods?
reviews.limitations: No, the paper lacks a dedicated section or paragraph discussing the shortcomings of the proposed method. Maybe discussing potential failure cases in real-world scenarios (like extreme occlusions) would make the evaluation much more comprehensive and honest.
reviews.final_justification: My concerns are almost resolved by the rebuttal. Taking overall consideration of comments from other reviewers, I am leaning to acceptance and would like to keep my original score.

reviews.summary:
This work explores the use of semi-supervised gaze estimation to improve the generation performance.

The evaluations are performed on some small-scale benchmarks and show improved performance.
reviews.strengths_and_weaknesses:
+ Overall the paper is technical sound.

+ The authors provide some interesting comparisons, including visualisations to show the effectiveness of the proposed method.
reviews.key_questions_for_authors:
- The related work section is simply a list of some related works, the analysis and discussions appear to be limited. In addition, the major differences between this work and existing works are not being clearly highlighted.

- In method section, it would be better to have a notation section detailing the maths symbols and operators presented in the paper. For example, what are scalars, vectors and matrices, etc. In addition, some of the maths operations are not being introduced or explained before using them.

- The novelty of this work appears to be limited. What are the existing challenges in the literature, why existing methods cannot address these issues, and how the proposed method addresses these, and how to well position this work in the current literature.

- The theoretical aspects of this work appear to be weak and not well established.

- In fig 2 what is the purpose of showing the Jacobian matrix? The analysis and discussions on this figure also appear to be limited.

- Fig. 3 and 4 are unclear to reviewer, it would be better to have some descriptions in caption with some insights to highlight the major differences.

- This work introduces many hyperparameters, it is unclear to reviewer how the hyperparameters are selected. Also the amount of parameter tuning efforts would limit the impact of this work, and the practical aspects of this work would be very limited.

- It would be better to have a section for limitations of this work and outline some future research directions.
reviews.limitations: Not presented in the main paper.
reviews.final_justification: After careful consideration of the paper and the authors' responses, the concerns have been partially addressed. I have revised my score to 3, which I believe best matches the merit of this work. No further questions to the authors.
